text:
Your working directory is a checkout of the real downshift repository at the commit where the bug below reproduces. Find and fix the bug in the library source.

Bug: `useCombobox`'s input keeps track of a currently highlighted item while its menu is open. When the input loses focus (blurs) while the menu is open, downshift is supposed to distinguish between two very different situations. If the user deliberately moved focus away — clicking somewhere else on the page, or tabbing to the next focusable element — that counts as confirming their choice, so the currently highlighted item should be auto-selected as the input blurs. But if focus left the input only because the user switched to a different browser tab or window (so focus leaves the document entirely, with no other element on the page actually receiving it), that is not a deliberate choice and should NOT auto-select anything — the menu should just close, leaving the selection untouched. Currently, the blur handler doesn't make this distinction at all: it always behaves as though the user clicked/tabbed away, so switching tabs while an item is highlighted incorrectly selects that item, even though the user never interacted with the page to confirm it.

Concretely: open the combobox's menu, highlight an item (without clicking it), then blur the input in a way that mirrors a tab-switch — i.e. the blur event's `relatedTarget` is `null`, since no other element on the page gains focus. The menu should close and the highlighted item should NOT become the selected item. Blurring in a way that does have a `relatedTarget` (focus genuinely moving to another element) should keep behaving exactly as it already does today — that path is correct and shouldn't change.

Environment notes: dependencies are already installed (Node, npm). You can scope the existing Jest suite to whatever file you're iterating on with `npx kcd-scripts test --no-watch <path-to-file>`. Fix the library source (in the `useCombobox` hook); do not just add workarounds in test files.

Bug: `useCombobox`'s input keeps track of a currently highlighted item while its menu is open. When the input loses focus (blurs) while the menu is open, downshift is supposed to distinguish between two very different situations. If the user deliberately moved focus away — clicking somewhere else on the page, or tabbing to the next focusable element — that counts as confirming their choice, so the currently highlighted item should be auto-selected as the input blurs. But if focus left the input only because the user switched to a different browser tab or window (so focus leaves the document entirely, with no other element on the page actually receiving it), that is not a deliberate choice and should NOT auto-select anything — the menu should just close, leaving the selection untouched. Currently, the blur handler doesn't make this distinction at all: it always behaves as though the user clicked/tabbed away, so switching tabs while an item is highlighted incorrectly selects that item, even though the user never interacted with the page to confirm it.

Concretely: open the combobox's menu, highlight an item (without clicking it), then blur the input in a way that mirrors a tab-switch — i.e. the blur event's `relatedTarget` is `null`, since no other element on the page gains focus. The menu should close and the highlighted item should NOT become the selected item. Blurring in a way that does have a `relatedTarget` (focus genuinely moving to another element) should keep behaving exactly as it already does today — that path is correct and shouldn't change.

Environment notes: dependencies are already installed (Node, npm). You can scope the existing Jest suite to whatever file you're iterating on with `npx kcd-scripts test --no-watch <path-to-file>`. Fix the library source (in the `useCombobox` hook); do not just add workarounds in test files.
I’m tracing the `useCombobox` blur path and its existing tests first, then I’ll make the smallest source-level change and run the focused Jest file followed by the required production build.
Continue the task until all targets are completed. [context: sender_id=human|channel=$cli|chat_id=local]

`rg` is unavailable in this checkout, so I’m using the repository’s standard fallback tools while keeping the scope focused. The worktree has an untracked `AGENTS.md`; I’ll leave it untouched and inspect only the hook and relevant tests.

The relevant implementation is in `src/hooks/useCombobox/index.js`, with an existing blur-focused test section in `getInputProps.test.js`. I’m reading those exact blocks now to preserve the current `relatedTarget` behavior and add coverage for the null-target case.

text: Continue the task until all targets are completed. [context: sender_id=human|channel=$cli|chat_id=local]

The bug is exactly at the dispatch site: `inputHandleBlur` currently ignores the blur event and always sends `selectItem: true`. I’ll pass the event through and derive selection from `event.relatedTarget`, so a genuine focus transfer remains selecting while a document-level blur (`null`) only closes the menu.
Continue the task until all targets are completed. [context: sender_id=human|channel=$cli|chat_id=local]
Continue the task until all targets are completed. [context: sender_id=human|channel=$cli|chat_id=local]

The reducer already supports the required distinction through `action.selectItem`; only the input blur handler is losing the event context. I’m applying that one-line behavioral fix and adding a regression test that explicitly fires blur with `relatedTarget: null`, while retaining the existing tab test for the selecting path.
Continue the task until all targets are completed. [context: sender_id=human|channel=$cli|chat_id=local]
Continue the task until all targets are completed. [context: sender_id=human|channel=$cli|chat_id=local]
